analogy: Think of "Human Health and Disease" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 7 Human Health and Disease Chapter 8 Microbes in Human Welfare Biology is the youngest of the formalised disciplines of natural science. Progress in physics and chemistry proceeded much faster than in Biology. Applications of physics and chemistry in our daily life also have... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Human Health and Disease is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that chapter 7 Human Health and Disease Chapter 8 Microbes in Human Welfare Biology is the youngest of the formalised disciplines of natural science.

The text explains that progress in physics and chemistry proceeded much faster than in Biology.

The text explains that applications of physics and chemistry in our daily life also have a higher visibility than those of biology.

The text explains that however, twentieth century and certainly twenty-first century has demonstrated the utility of biological knowledge in furthering human welfare, be it in health sector or agriculture.

The text explains that the discovery of antibiotics, and synthetic plant-derived drugs, anaesthetics have changed medical practice on one hand and human health on the other hand.

The text explains that life expectancy of human beings have dramatically changed over the years.

The text explains that agricultural practices, food processing and diagnostics have brought socio-cultural changes in human communities.

The text explains that these are briefly described in the following three chapters of this unit.

The text explains that born in August 1925 in Kumbakonam in Tamil Nadu, Monkambu Sambasivan Swaminathan did his graduation and post-graduation in Botany from Madras University.

The text explains that he worked in different capacities in large number of institutions in India and abroad and developed his expertise in genetics and plant breeding.

The text explains that the School of Cytogenetics and Radiation Research established at the Indian Agricultural Research Institute (IARI) enabled Swaminathan and his team to develop short-duration high-yielding varieties of rice including scented Basmati.

The text explains that he is also known for the development of the concept of crop cafeteria, crop scheduling and genetically improving the yield and quality.

The text explains that swaminathan initiated collaboration with Norman Borlaug, which culminated in the ‘Green Revolution’ through introduction of Mexican varieties of wheat in India.

The text explains that he is also the initiator of ‘Lab-to-Land’, food security and several other environmental programmes.

The text explains that he has been honoured with Padma Bhushan and several other prestigious awards, medals and fellowships by institutions of excellence.

The text explains that health, for a long time, was considered as a state of body and mind where there was a balance of certain ‘humors’.

The text explains that the discovery of blood circulation by William Harvey using experimental method and the demonstration of normal body temperature in persons with blackbile using thermometer disproved the ‘good humor’ hypothesis of health.

The text explains that awareness about diseases and their effect on different bodily functions, vaccination (immunisation) against infectious diseases, proper disposal of wastes, control of vectors and maintenance of hygiene in food and water resources are necessary for achieving good health.

The text explains that droplets resulting from cough or sneezes of an infected person are either inhaled directly or transmitted through contaminated objects such as pens, books, cups, doorknobs, computer keyboard or mouse, etc., and cause infection in a healthy person.

The text explains that drinking water and food contaminated by the faecal matter are the main source of infection.

The text explains that 136 BIOLOGY just any source – an animal, another primate, or any human beings cannot be made since the grafts would be rejected sooner or later.

The text explains that when ready-made antibodies are directly given to protect the body against foreign agents, it is called passive immunity.

The text explains that if a person is infected with some deadly microbes to which quick immune response is required as in tetanus, we need to directly inject the preformed antibodies, or antitoxin (a preparation containing antibodies to the toxin).

The text explains that this viral DNA gets incorporated into host cell’s DNA and directs the infected cells to produce virus particles (Figure 7.6).

The text explains that nicotine stimulates adrenal gland to release adrenaline and nor-adrenaline into blood circulation, both of which raise blood pressure and increase heart rate.

The text explains that of late, stress, from pressures to excel in academics or examinations, has played a significant role in persuading the youngsters to try alcohol and drugs.

The text explains that other factors that have been seen to be associated with drug and alcohol abuse among adolescents are unstable or unsupportive family structures and peer pressure.

The text explains that 146 BIOLOGY Those who take drugs intravenously (direct injection into the vein using a needle and syringe), are much more likely to acquire serious infections like AIDS and Hepatitis B.

The text explains that some of the measures mentioned here would be particularly useful for prevention and control of alcohol and drugs abuse among adolescents (i) Avoid undue peer pressure - Every child has his/her own choice and personality, which should be respected and nurtured.

The text explains that because of the addictive nature of alcohol and drugs, and their perceived benefits like relief from stress, a person may try taking these in the face of peer pressure, examinations-related and competition-related stresses.

The text explains that differentiate the following and give examples of each: (a) Innate and acquired immunity (b) Active and passive immunity 9.

The text explains that draw a well-labelled diagram of an antibody molecule.

The text explains that what are the various routes by which transmission of human immuno- deficiency virus takes place?

The text explains that what is the mechanism by which the AIDS virus causes deficiency of immune system of the infected person?

The text explains that how is a cancerous cell different from a normal cell?

The text explains that list the harmful effects caused by alcohol/drug abuse.

The text explains that do you think that friends can influence one to take alcohol/drugs?

The text explains that if yes, how may one protect himself/herself from such an influence?

The text explains that why is that once a person starts taking alcohol or drugs, it is difficult to get rid of this habit?

The text explains that in your view what motivates youngsters to take to alcohol or drugs and how can this be avoided?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7: Human Health and Disease

prelims_traps: Do not treat M.S. SWAMINATHAN and CHAPTER 7 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Health and Disease; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Human Health and Disease through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use M.S. SWAMINATHAN and CHAPTER 7 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
